_metadata.description: Comprehensive database of harmful additives, contaminants, and concerning ingredients found in dietary supplements. Used for scoring penalties and consumer warnings.
_metadata.scoring_rule: Apply penalty points based on severity_level. Critical: -5, High: -3, Moderate: -1.5, Low: -0.5. Multiple additives are cumulative.

severity_level_definitions.critical: Immediate health hazard, should be avoided entirely (e.g., formaldehyde, colloidal silver)
severity_level_definitions.high: Significant health concerns with strong evidence (e.g., trans fats, carcinogenic dyes, heavy metals)
severity_level_definitions.moderate: Potential health concerns, limit exposure (e.g., artificial sweeteners, high glycemic fillers)
severity_level_definitions.low: Minor concerns, generally safe at typical doses (e.g., some flow agents, natural thickeners)

fields.aliases: Alternative names, E-numbers, chemical names for matching
fields.mechanism_of_harm: Scientific explanation of how the additive causes harm
fields.regulatory_status: Approval status in US, EU, and other jurisdictions
fields.notes: Additional context and recent research findings
fields.references_structured: Structured citations with authority and grade

_metadata.pipeline_integration: Used by enrichment script to apply penalty points for harmful ingredients during scoring. Matches against ingredient names and other_ingredient forms.
sources: PubMed peer-reviewed studies (DOIs provided per entry)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Contains hydroxyanthracene derivatives (HADs) including cascarosides. FDA formally ruled cascara sagrada 'not generally recognized as safe and effective' in 2002 (67 FR 31125). EFSA 2018/2024: HADs are genotoxic and carcinogenic concern. Same chronic risks as senna: laxative dependency, electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia), melanosis coli, liver damage. NIH LiverTox documents acute liver failure cases.

regulatory_status.US: FDA stripped GRASE status in 2002 (67 FR 31125). Added to 21 CFR 310.545 (not safe/effective for OTC laxative use). Still sold in supplements under DSHEA regulatory gap.
regulatory_status.EU: EFSA 2018/2024: HADs genotoxic concern. Same regulatory trajectory as senna.
regulatory_status.Canada: Health Canada permits for max 7 days with mandatory warnings.

population_warnings: Pregnant women - Contraindicated
Children - Not recommended
People with inflammatory bowel disease - Contraindicated
People with electrolyte imbalances - Risk of hypokalemia
Chronic users - Laxative dependency and colonic atony risk
harmful_additives.notes: FDA explicitly stripped GRASE status in 2002 after industry failed to submit required safety data. Only remains in supplements due to DSHEA regulatory gap, NOT because it was found safe. EFSA 2018/2024: HADs genotoxic and carcinogenic concern. NIH LiverTox documents liver injury cases including acute liver failure with portal hypertension. Stronger regulatory case against it than senna.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Germanium compounds cause renal tubular degeneration leading to irreversible kidney failure. At least 31 documented cases of renal failure and 2 deaths from chronic oral germanium intake (doses 15-300g over 2-36 months). Both organic (Ge-132) and inorganic (GeO2) forms implicated; organic products often contaminated with nephrotoxic GeO2. Damage persists after discontinuation; some patients required permanent dialysis.

regulatory_status.US: FDA Import Alert 54-07: ALL germanium supplements detained as 'poisonous or deleterious substance' under Section 801(a)(3). One step below formal ban.
regulatory_status.EU: Effectively prohibited — germanium not listed in Annex I/II of Directive 2002/46/EC (permitted minerals for food supplements)
regulatory_status.UK: Not included in Schedule 1 of permitted substances for food supplements

population_warnings: ALL populations - FDA classifies as 'poisonous or deleterious substance'
People with any renal impairment - Extreme risk of irreversible kidney failure
Elderly populations - Reduced renal clearance amplifies nephrotoxic risk
Pregnant or nursing women - Insufficient safety data; organ toxicity risk
harmful_additives.notes: FDA Import Alert 54-07 authorizes border detention of ALL germanium supplements without physical examination. Memorial Sloan Kettering states 'should not be consumed because they can cause renal, hepatic, and neurotoxicities.' The distinction between pure Ge-132 and contaminated/inorganic forms is debated (Reddeman 2020 vs Tao & Bolger 1997), but consumer-facing products cannot guarantee purity, and the consequences (irreversible renal failure, death) justify high severity.

PMID: 1726409 - Schauss, Biol Trace Elem Res 1991;29(3):267-280 (18 renal failure cases)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Petroleum-derived lubricant containing mineral oil saturated hydrocarbons (MOSH) and mineral oil aromatic hydrocarbons (MOAH). EFSA 2023 risk assessment found MOAH with 3+ aromatic rings are genotoxic and associated with DNA damage and carcinogenicity. MOSH can accumulate in human tissues. Oral mineral oil impairs absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), directly counterproductive in a supplement context.

regulatory_status.US: FDA permitted under 21 CFR 172.878 with purity specifications limiting aromatic content.
regulatory_status.EU: Under active regulatory restriction. EU Commission draft regulation (late 2023) proposes maximum MOAH levels in food, expected adoption 2025 with applicability from 2027. France banned MOAH-containing mineral oils in food packaging inks as of Jan 2025.
regulatory_status.WHO: No specific position on supplement use; defers to national regulators.

population_warnings: People taking fat-soluble vitamin supplements (A, D, E, K) - May impair absorption
Children - Higher MOAH exposure concern per EFSA
Elderly - Risk of lipoid pneumonia from aspiration
harmful_additives.notes: EFSA 2023 assessment concluded MOAH fractions with 3+ aromatic rings pose a 'possible concern for human health.' While MOSH was found not to raise concerns at current levels, the safety margin is narrow. Mineral oil is an avoidable excipient -- better-formulated supplements use plant-based alternatives like magnesium stearate or vegetable-based release agents. EU is actively moving to restrict MOAH in food.

Acts as a stimulant laxative causing chronic dependency, electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia leading to cardiac arrhythmias), melanosis coli, and documented liver injury including acute liver failure.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Polyphosphate salt contributing significant inorganic phosphorus absorbed at 90-100% efficiency (vs 40-60% for natural food phosphorus). EFSA 2019 re-evaluation established group ADI of 40 mg phosphorus/kg bw/day, which is already exceeded in infants, toddlers, and children at mean intake levels. Elevated serum phosphorus is associated with increased cardiovascular disease, vascular calcification, and accelerated kidney disease progression even within the 'normal' lab range. Approximately 10% of the general population has some degree of CKD with reduced renal function.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS as sequestrant when used per good manufacturing practice (21 CFR 182).
regulatory_status.EU: E452i approved. EFSA 2019 re-evaluation set group ADI of 40 mg P/kg bw/day for all phosphate additives (E 338-452). ADI already exceeded in children.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA established maximum tolerable daily intake for phosphorus.

population_warnings: Children, infants, and toddlers - EFSA found ADI already exceeded in these groups at mean intake
People with chronic kidney disease (CKD) - ~10% of general population; cannot efficiently excrete phosphorus
People with cardiovascular disease - Elevated phosphorus linked to vascular calcification
Elderly - Higher CKD prevalence
harmful_additives.notes: While SHMP has low acute toxicity, the inorganic phosphorus it contributes is a legitimate public health concern. EFSA's 2019 re-evaluation was the first time a numerical ADI was set for phosphates (previously 'not specified'), reflecting growing evidence of harm. A supplement intended to promote health should not add unnecessary inorganic phosphorus, especially given that the ADI is already exceeded in vulnerable populations.
